senses.definition: plural of scalding; hot liquid splashes or boiling liquids that burn.
examples: The scaldings from the kettle burned his skin.
Be careful of the scaldings when pouring hot water.

senses.definition: a plural form that may refer to small scalding vessels or vessels named after an Italian-sounding term; not a common English word.
examples: The term scaldini appears in some niche texts about traditional cookware.
Scaldini were displayed in the museum exhibit.

senses.definition: an open-tinished vessel used to hold hot liquids; sometimes a small brazier.
examples: A scaldino kept the coals warm at the table.
They placed the scaldino between dishes to keep soup hot.

senses.definition: uncommon or invented term; not a standard English lexical item.
examples: Scaldra is not widely used in English today.
In some creative texts, scaldra appears as a mythical object.

senses.definition: plural of scald; burns caused by hot liquids or steam.
examples: He suffered several scalds from boiling water.
Cold water helped heal the scalds on his arm.

senses.definition: to burn with hot liquid or steam; to scald someone or something.
examples: Do not scald your fingers when removing the lid.
The milk will scald if left unattended.

senses.definition: uncommon or invented term; not part of standard English.
examples: Scaldship appears only in certain fantasy glossaries.
The author invented scaldship to describe a fiery alliance.

senses.definition: plural of scaldship; a fictional or rare term in English.
examples: The scaldships glowed red in the dusk.

senses.definition: a plant name used in folklore or as a specialized term; not common in modern English.
examples: The herbalist mentioned scaldweed in her notes.
Scaldweed leaves were said to treat burns in old lore.

senses.definition: slightly scalded or burned; used to describe a burn in progress.
examples: The vegetables were scaldy after the boilover.
Her fingers looked scaldy from the steam.

senses.definition: any of the hard, thin plates that cover the skin of some animals or the small flat pieces on a fish or dragon; a device for measuring weight.
examples: A fish has slippery scales.
The scale on the kitchen shows the weight.
This dragon statue has large scales.

senses.definition: to climb or go up a scale; to remove scale from a surface; to adjust in size or scope.
examples: The climber will scale the cliff.
We scaled the fish to remove the scales.
Sales have scaled back this quarter.

senses.definition: able to be scaled or expanded to handle more workload or size.
examples: The system is scalable to handle more users.
We need a scalable solution for future growth.

senses.definition: in a scalable manner; in a way that can be expanded.
examples: The service can scaleably handle peak traffic.
The design is scaleably deployed across regions.

senses.definition: a back or part of a surface that has been scaled or scaled down; can refer to a back or a support that has scales removed.
examples: The turtle's scaleback was smooth after shedding.
In carpentry, a scaleback edge may need refinishing.

senses.definition: bark that has a scaly texture; or a term used in botany or heraldry.
examples: The scalebark texture is rough to touch.
In old heraldry, scalebark patterns appeared on shields.

senses.definition: a board with a scale; or a board used for scaling measurements.
examples: The scaleboard showed the ratio clearly.
We use a scaleboard when calibrating equipment.

senses.definition: plural of scaleboard; boards used for scaling measurements.
examples: The lab inspected the scaleboards before the test.

senses.definition: past tense of scale; to climb or to remove scales; to adjust in size.
examples: The climber scaled the cliff.
We scaled the fish to clean it.
The project was scaled to fit the budget.

senses.definition: described as having been scaled; covered with scales or adjusted in size.
examples: Scaled armor appeared on the dragon statue.
The scaled model was accurate to the original.

senses.definition: a specialized tool; or a coined term in niche contexts.
examples: The gardener used a scaledrake to loosen soil.
In fantasy writing, scaledrake refers to a mythical implement.

senses.definition: a fish with prominent scales; could refer to a species or common name in cookery.
examples: The scalefish is common in coastal waters.
We prepared scalefish fillets for dinner.

senses.definition: full of scales or having many scales.
examples: The scaleful skin of the lizard was shimmering.
A scaleful surface is often slippery.

senses.definition: without scales; smooth or bare.
examples: A scaleless fish skin feels slick.
The reptile is scaleless in some mutated form.

senses.definition: a small scale; a minor scale element.
examples: The scalelet on the dragon's wing glowed.
Each scalelet contributed to the overall texture.

senses.definition: resembling or having the characteristics of scales.
examples: The reptile has scalelike scales.
Her skin looked scalelike under the microscope.

senses.definition: a person who scales things; or a playful or historical term for a worker who handles scales.
examples: The scaleman climbed the cliff with ropes.
In old texts, a scaleman might refer to a fish scaler.

senses.definition: plural of scaleman; workers who remove scales or who scale cliffs, depending on context.
examples: The scalemen worked by the riverside, cleaning fish.
In the old cannery, scalemen prepared fish.
